source: .specs/agent-scenarios.md → Feature: Multiple agents in same workspace
notes: KNOWN FAILING — agentB_turn_done fails when opencode is agentB: opencode is driven headlessly via `opencode run`, which EXITS at turn-end, so the working->ready settle races the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore poller. Under 5-way concurrent load the process_exited fires ~140 ms after the final transcript_activity, before the poller reads step-finish=stop and emits working->ready (cf. pi/turn-aborted-by-error: headless fast-exit truncation; basic-turn captures the settle standalone because the poller's tick wins the race when opencode runs alone). The LOAD-BEARING assertions all pass: opencode appears as a DISTINCT session (new_session) that enters working independently in the SAME cwd as a different adapter — i.e. two agents, one workspace, labeled with no cross-contamination. A live-REPL re-record that keeps the process alive across the settle is impossible: the opencode driver uses single-shot `opencode run` (capabilities headless_mode=false; the -i TUI is not driver-supported). Clears if the daemon gains sub-poll-interval ProcessOwnedStore settle detection before process_exited, or if the driver gains a persistent-session mode that outlives the turn. Original rationale follows. One opencode session and at least one different-adapter session driven concurrently in the SAME cwd by run-cell-multi.sh, observed by a single --record daemon. opencode lives in a ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) keyed by directory; the partner adapters live in disjoint FilesUnderRoot/FilesUnderCWD transports, so the daemon labels each adapter independently and the workspace events.jsonl carries distinct sessions — one per adapter — each with its own working->ready arc and no cross-contamination. This spec is adapter-agnostic: agentA is whichever session enters working first, agentB the other; this opencode cell stores the opencode transcript for the replay golden and shares the workspace events.jsonl with the other cross_adapter cells. Every phase anchors on a WORKING transition, never a ready, because the process scanner emits a pre-session proc-<PID> row born in ready that never works — anchoring on working skips those placeholders, and new_session on agentB skips agentA's already-matched session. opencode's default model is a slow local LM Studio model (~30-45s/turn), so agentA's first-to-working may be a faster partner agent and opencode may be agentB; the spec is symmetric and validates either ordering. Asserts: two distinct sessions, each an independent working->ready arc, in one workspace.

text: First agent's turn drives ready -> working (binds agentA; skips both adapters' born-ready proc-<PID> placeholders by anchoring on working). Wide window absorbs opencode's slow local model.
First agent's turn completes: working -> ready
The OTHER agent (a DISTINCT session, different adapter, same cwd) drives ready -> working independently (new_session proves it is not agentA; working anchor skips the placeholders)
Second agent's turn completes: working -> ready — two agents, one workspace, labeled independently with no cross-contamination
